rows.title: Italian Garante Issues Guidelines on Tracking Pixels in Email Communications
rows.summary:
Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has adopted guidelines governing the use of tracking pixels in email communications, establishing legal requirements applicable to platform operators, service providers and intermediaries under the GDPR (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) and the e-Privacy Directive (2002/58/CE as amended by 2009/136/CE).

On 17 April 2026, the Garante, in plenary session with President Pasquale Stanzione, Vice-President Ginevra Cerrina Feroni and member Agostino Ghiglia, adopted Provvedimento n. 284, published under doc. web n. 10241943 and pending publication in the Gazzetta Ufficiale. The Garante describes tracking pixels as very small, often transparent images of a single pixel embedded in email messages, and finds them to be a category of tracking tool that is particularly invasive due to their concealed nature, which renders them unidentifiable by the data subject.

The Garante notes a marked increase in the use of tracking and behavioural monitoring tools across digital environments, alongside a growing volume of requests received daily from individuals concerning the existence, characteristics and effects of such tools. The guidelines are framed within a body of prior regulatory instruments, including the Garante's 2021 cookie and tracking tool guidelines (Provvedimento n. 231 of 10 June 2021), the 2015 online profiling guidelines (Provvedimento n. 161 of 19 March 2015), and the EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 of 7 October 2024 on the technical scope of Art. 5(3) of the e-Privacy Directive. The Garante also references EDPB Opinion 05/2019 on the interrelationship between the e-Privacy Directive and the GDPR, and WP29 Opinion 05/2014 on anonymisation techniques.

The Garante determines that tracking pixels, alongside other tracking instruments such as cookies, fingerprinting, web beacons, scripts, JavaScript, mouseflow tools and widgets, require specific protective measures that account for their distinct technical characteristics. The Garante states that the capacity of individuals to exercise control over their personal data, particularly in digital contexts, is a prerequisite for trust in the digital ecosystem. The guidelines apply to operators deploying tracking pixels in electronic mail communications and are directed at ensuring that such processing meets the requirements of the GDPR and the Italian Data Protection Code (d.lgs. n. 196/2003, as amended by d.lgs. n. 101/2018).
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Garante Issues Opinion on Draft Decree Governing Telematic Agency Register Platform at Italy's Directorate General for Motorisation
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued an opinion on a draft decree prepared by the Directorate General for Motorisation of the Italian Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport, governing the telematic platform used for registration in the Electronic Register of Telematic Agencies (ReAT), on the basis of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) and Legislative Decree No. 196 of 30 June 2003 (the Italian Data Protection Code).

On 17 April 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted the opinion, registered as provvedimento No. 232 of 26 March 2026, following consultations requested by the Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport through notes dated 14 November 2025, 19 February 2026 and 20 March 2026. The opinion concerns the platform established at the data processing centre of the Directorate General for Motorisation, which handles the timing and modalities for the submission of registration applications by telematic agencies seeking inscription in the ReAT.

The Garante notes that the ReAT framework derives from Law No. 177 of 25 November 2024, which established the register for firms providing professional consultancy services for the circulation of motor vehicles pursuant to Law No. 264 of 8 August 1991. The Garante further notes that, under Decree No. 200 of 16 June 2025 issued by the Head of Department, registration in the ReAT constitutes a necessary condition for access to and operation within the automated information systems of the Directorate General for Motorisation. The Garante finds that applications for ReAT registration are submitted via a dedicated web application accessed through SPID level 2 or the Electronic Identity Card (CIE), and that suspension, revocation or cancellation of ReAT registration precludes the agency from accessing those systems.

The Garante finds that the draft decree addresses the specific procedural aspects delegated by Decree No. 200 of 2025, namely the timing and modalities for submission of registration requests through the telematic platform. The Garante's opinion was adopted in the session attended by President Pasquale Stanzione, Vice-President Ginevra Cerrina Feroni, Member Agostino Ghiglia, and Secretary General Luigi Montuori, in application of the applicable provisions of the GDPR (Art. 36 and Art. 58) and the Italian Data Protection Code.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Italy's ACN Publishes NIS Compliance Determinations for New Entities and Platform Access Rules
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale (ACN) has published two determinations establishing compliance timelines for entities newly registered under the NIS framework in 2026 and updating access rules for the ACN digital platform, including provisions on the listing of relevant suppliers.

On 13 April 2026, the Agenzia per la Cybersicurezza Nazionale published Determination 127434/2026 and Determination 127437/2026, both examined at the eighth meeting of the NIS Roundtable and made available on ACN's institutional website. ACN states that no financial penalties are associated with these determinations.

ACN states that the first application phase under Article 42 of the NIS Decree concluded at the end of 2025. Determination 127434/2026 establishes the compliance timelines applicable to entities entered for the first time in the NIS register in 2026. ACN finds that the obligation to notify significant incidents, as defined under the baseline specifications set out in Determination 379907/2025, begins on 1 January 2027 for these new entities. Consequently, ACN notes that the designation of a CSIRT contact point is required by the end of 2026. ACN states that the adoption of baseline security measures is required by July 2027 for these newly registered entities.

Determination 127437/2026 updates the terms of use and access to the ACN digital platform and, ACN notes, introduces Article 18 concerning the listing of relevant suppliers. ACN states that this exercise is necessary to identify, from among suppliers, those to be designated as important or essential entities, with the aim of promoting an adequate level of cybersecurity across the supply chains of NIS entities, as provided under Article 3, paragraph 9, letter f) of the NIS Decree. ACN states that the determination also introduces, at Articles 20 and 21, procedural rules governing the listing and categorisation of activities and services in accordance with Article 30 of the NIS Decree.

ACN further states that a determination containing the categorisation model will be published the following week, together with supporting materials to assist entities in conducting a simplified business impact analysis (BIA) during May and June 2026, developed through the sectoral roundtables. ACN notes that the dedicated NIS web area on its institutional site will be updated in the coming days to reflect these developments.

rows.title: Italian Garante Reprimands Municipality for Unlawful Disclosure of Employee Sick-Leave Certificate
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a reprimand against an Italian municipality for disclosing the full electronic sick-leave certificate of its secretary-general to external and internal recipients without a valid legal basis, in violation of Arts. 5(1)(a), 5(1)(c), 6 and 9 of the GDPR and Art. 2-ter of the Italian Privacy Code (d.lgs. 196/2003).

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted decision no. 201, finding that the municipality had processed health-related personal data unlawfully when it attached a copy of the complainant's electronic sick-leave certificate to a formal request addressed to the Prefettura and to the secretary-general of a second municipality, seeking authorisation for a temporary replacement during the complainant's absence.

The Garante finds that the municipality transmitted the certificate to recipients that the complainant contended had no entitlement to access it, and that the document was also accessed internally by a protocol officer and staff in the secretariat. During the investigation, the municipality acknowledged that attachment of the certificate had been an error, stating that the employee who prepared the communication had been assigned from another office as a temporary substitute and that previous requests of the same kind had never included such attachments (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR). The municipality further represented that the Prefettura, as the body responsible for managing the register of municipal secretaries and the relevant personnel files, was authorised to process the complainant's personal data, and that the certificate had been forwarded not to the general address of the second municipality but solely to the certified email address of the replacement secretary. The municipality also contended that the certificate, by its design, contained no diagnostic information and disclosed only a generic attestation of absence.

The Garante finds that these circumstances do not eliminate the unlawfulness of the processing. The Garante determines that transmission of the certificate to external recipients and its internal circulation occurred in the absence of an adequate legal basis and in breach of the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency and data minimisation (Arts. 5(1)(a) and 5(1)(c) GDPR). The Garante finds that the processing of data classifiable as health data required a specific legal basis under Art. 9 GDPR, which was not established on the facts. The Garante notes that the municipality did not request a hearing before the authority during the proceedings conducted under Art. 166(5) of the Privacy Code.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Italian Garante Fines Fix It S.R.L.S. for Unlawful Marketing Emails and Failure to Respond to Access Request
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a corrective decision against Fix It S.r.l.s., operator of the platform www.megamta.com, finding violations of the GDPR and the Italian Privacy Code arising from unsolicited commercial communications sent without a valid legal basis and a failure to respond to a data subject access request.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted decision no. 211, registering the matter under document reference 10241455. The proceedings originated from a complaint lodged on 27 September 2025 by an individual who reported receiving an unauthorised commercial email from Fix It S.r.l.s., a company registered in Borgo Veneto, Padua, and who alleged that his access request submitted on 30 July 2025 had received no reply.

The Garante finds that Fix It S.r.l.s. processed the complainant's personal data for marketing purposes without being able to demonstrate a lawful basis for that processing, in breach of Art. 5(1)(a), Art. 5(2), and Art. 6 GDPR, as well as Art. 130 of the Italian Privacy Code (Legislative Decree 196/2003, as amended). The Garante determines that the company's general assertion that its platform is intended solely to send communications to recipients who have accepted and confirmed they wish to receive them from MegaMTA does not constitute adequate evidence that the complainant's consent was obtained and relied upon as the legal basis for the processing in question. The Garante further finds that the company failed to respond to the complainant's access request within the required timeframe, in breach of Art. 12 and Art. 15 GDPR.

In its defence submissions of 23 February 2026, Fix It S.r.l.s. contended that it had not received the complainant's email exercising his rights and noted that the complainant had unsubscribed from its systems on 21 September 2025, after which no further commercial communications were sent. The company also indicated that data access is available at any time through the personal area of the MegaMTA portal, accessible via its email communications. The Garante determines that these arguments are insufficient to exclude liability for the contested violations, noting that no specific information was provided regarding the legal basis underpinning the processing of the complainant's data, and that, in the absence of such evidence, the processing is to be regarded as unlawful (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR).
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Italian Garante Orders Removal of Judicial Document Posted on Facebook, Finds Unlawful Processing of Criminal Data
rows.summary:
The Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has ruled against Nicolò Cristaldi, finding that his publication of a criminal penalty decree on his personal Facebook profile constituted unlawful processing of judicial personal data in breach of the GDPR and applicable Italian law, and ordering removal of the content.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali issued Provvedimento n. 215, following a complaint lodged on 30 July 2025 under Art. 77 GDPR by an individual identified as the subject of a criminal penalty decree. The Garante finds that Cristaldi published on his Facebook profile a document dated 21 July 2025 communicating the issuance of a decree of criminal conviction. The complainant argued that the publication constituted unlawful processing of judicial data without valid legal basis and was damaging to his reputation. An authority information request was sent to Cristaldi on 9 September 2025 pursuant to Art. 157 of the Italian Privacy Code, but received no response. The Garante finds that failure to respond constitutes an independent administrative infringement.

In defensive submissions filed on 12 February 2026, assisted by the Guardia di Finanza's Nucleo speciale privacy, Cristaldi stated he had not viewed the certified email sent by the authority, as it had been addressed to his company and subsequently deleted by the company's accountant. Cristaldi contended that the publication was lawful as an exercise of the right to report on matters of public interest, given the judicial proceedings involving the complainant. The Garante, however, finds that the publication breached the principles of lawfulness, fairness and data minimisation under Art. 5(1)(a) and (c) GDPR, as well as Art. 137 of the Code and Art. 6 of the Deontological Rules for journalistic processing of personal data, on the basis that the content exceeded what the principle of essentiality of information permits in journalistic activity.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: Garante Reprimands Il Gazzettino for Failure to Respond to Data Subject Rights Request
rows.summary:
Italy's Garante per la protezione dei dati personali has issued a reprimand against Il Gazzettino S.p.A. for failing to respond to a data subject's rights request within the statutory timeframe, finding a violation of Articles 5(2), 12(2) and (3), and 24(1) and (2) of the GDPR.

On 26 March 2026, the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali adopted Provvedimento n. 216, addressing a complaint filed pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR and formalised on 14 October 2025. The complainant, a general practitioner, alleged that Il Gazzettino S.p.A. had published an article identifying her by full name in connection with her refusal to continue treating two patients. The complainant submitted a preliminary request to the data controller on 29 July 2025, to which she received no response prior to filing her complaint.

The Garante finds that Il Gazzettino S.p.A. failed to provide any response to the complainant's exercise of rights within the one-month period required under Art. 12(3) GDPR. The authority noted the controller's explanation that the delay resulted from the complainant's use of a certified email address rather than the dedicated privacy contact address, compounded by the request arriving during the summer holiday period and the illness and death of the journalist responsible for the article. The Garante determined that these circumstances do not constitute sufficient justification for the failure to respond, as the controller bears accountability for ensuring that rights requests are managed effectively regardless of the channel used.

The authority acknowledged that following notification of the complaint, the controller removed the complainant's name from the digital archive version of the article and subsequently took the article offline entirely. The Garante finds that the inclusion of the complainant's full name was not essential to the informational purpose pursued by the article. The Garante determined that the decision takes the form of a reprimand under Art. 58(2)(b) GDPR with no financial penalty recorded.
rows.body_name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

rows.title: AgID Publishes Draft Guidelines for AI System Development in Italian Public Administration
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) has issued a draft version of guidelines governing the development of artificial intelligence systems within the Italian public administration, issued pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 on the Three-Year Plan for Information Technology in Public Administration 2024-2026.

On 11 March 2026, AgID published Version 1.0 of the draft guidelines, titled "Linee Guida per lo sviluppo di sistemi di Intelligenza Artificiale nella pubblica amministrazione", opened for public consultation. AgID states the document does not carry a financial penalty and is not a final binding instrument at this stage.

AgID sets out seven general principles drawn from Law 132/2025 that the guidelines state are applicable to the development and procurement of AI systems across the public administration. According to AgID, these principles cover: the protection of fundamental rights, transparency, proportionality and non-discrimination (Principle 1); the quality, accuracy, reliability and transparency of data and processes (Principle 2); human-centredness, explainability and harm prevention (Principle 3); the protection of democratic and institutional processes and sovereignty (Principle 4); coherence with the European regulatory framework, specifically the EU AI Act (Principle 5); cybersecurity and resilience across the system lifecycle (Principle 6); and universal accessibility and inclusion (Principle 7).

The draft guidelines also establish a reference logical architecture for AI systems in the public administration. AgID defines a layered AI stack encompassing an Energy Layer, a Chip Layer, an Infrastructure Layer, an AI Model Layer and an Application Layer. AgID further identifies a reference AI architecture model comprising AI models, heterogeneous data sources across cloud, on-premises and hybrid environments, application-level tools and an orchestrator component. AgID classifies AI system families to which the guidelines apply, including statistical or data-driven AI, machine learning, neural networks, deep learning and generative AI. AgID addresses the role of data in AI system development, including training data and operational data, as a distinct dimension of the reference architecture.

rows.title: Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale Publishes Draft Guidelines on AI Procurement for Italian Public Administrations
rows.summary:
Italy's Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale (AgID) has published draft guidelines on the procurement of artificial intelligence systems by public administrations, issued pursuant to the D.P.C.M. of 12 January 2024 establishing the three-year plan for public administration information technology 2024–2026.

On 11 March 2026, the Agenzia per l'Italia Digitale published Version 1.0 of the draft Linee guida per il procurement di IA nella Pubblica Amministrazione for public consultation. AgID states that the guidelines apply to all entities falling within the scope of Article 2, paragraph 2, of the Codice dell'Amministrazione Digitale (CAD), collectively referred to throughout the document as PA.

AgID sets out that the guidelines address the modalities for procuring AI systems with particular reference to regulatory compliance and organisational impact. AgID notes that the document is structured around five principal areas: the general framework for acquiring AI systems; metrics, costs, monitoring and contract management; and procurement instruments and inter-administrative cooperation. AgID identifies distinct families of AI systems for procurement purposes and introduces a Levelised Cost of AI (LCOAI) methodology as an alternative to traditional economic appraisal approaches, which AgID notes have limitations when applied to AI lifecycle costs.

AgID addresses cybersecurity risk management for AI procurement, including attack taxonomies and security objectives, and sets out considerations relating to hardware neutrality, the use of accelerators, and system portability. AgID also addresses the role of data in procurement decisions and the implications of architectural choices on cost, control and risk exposure. AgID notes that the document includes provisions on interoperability and cooperation between public administrations, as well as good practices applicable across the full contract lifecycle. AgID presents the guidelines as a draft open to public consultation prior to formal adoption.
